channel.title: synesis
channel.description: AI research, engineering, and the occasional philosophical aside.

item.title: Document Intelligence Workshop @ KDD 2022: Program Announced
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Announcing the program of the 3rd Document Intelligence Workshop @ KDD2022 (DI-2022)!

  • DI-2022 Program: https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2022/program/

  • DI-2022 website: https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2022/

  • Program Committee Chair: Ani Nenkova (Adobe)

  • Organizing committee: Doug Burdick (IBM Research), Benjamin Han (Microsoft), Dave Lewis (Redgrave Data), Sandeep Tata (Google), and Dan Tecuci (EY).

  • Previous year (DI-2021): https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2021/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: DLG4NLP Keynote @ NAACL 2022: GNN for NLP (Zheng Zhang)
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Professor Zheng Zhang (New York University, Amazon Web Services (AWS)) gave a very insightful keynote talk at DLG4NLP Workshop @ NAACL2022 on caling out GNN Applications for NLP:

  • Development of Deep Graph Library (DGL).
  • Mapping between text and graphs.
  • Two discourse tasks: coreference resolution and discourse relations.
  • KBQA.

I’m also in strong agreement with Professor Zhang that the future belongs to the joined forces of neural and symbolic methods.

DLG4NLP Workshop @ NAACL2022: https://dlg4nlp-workshop.github.io/dlg4nlp-naacl22

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Brain-Computer Interface: First Stentrode Implant in US
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

“A doctor makes an incision in the patient’s neck and feeds the stentrode via a catheter through the jugular vein into a blood vessel nestled within the motor cortex. As the catheter is removed, the stentrode—a cylindrical, hollow wire mesh—opens up and begins to fuse with the outer edges of the vessel. According to Majidi, the process is very similar to implanting a coronary stent and takes just a few minutes.

A second procedure then connects the stentrode via a wire to a computing device implanted in the patient’s chest. To do this, the surgeon must create a tunnel for the wire and a pocket for the device underneath the patient’s skin, much like what’s done to accommodate a pacemaker. The stentrode reads the signals when neurons fire in the brain, and the computing device amplifies those signals and sends them out to a computer or smartphone via Bluetooth.”

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Hajishirzi on Knowledge-Rich General-Purpose NLP @ NAACL 2022
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Professor Hannaneh Hajishirzi (University of Washington, Allen Institute for AI (AI2)) gave an invited talk at SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022 on building general-purpose and knowledge-rich NLP models.

  • Meta-ICL uses meta-training to learn tasks from demonstrations. https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.15943
  • TK-Instruct uses knowledge from instructions of related tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.07705
  • GKP uses prompting to generate knowledge from LLMs. https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.08387

SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022: https://suki-workshop.github.io/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Our SUKI @ NAACL 2022 Paper on Few-Shot Intent/Slot Learning
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Job done for our paper at SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022 today, Samyadeep Basu & Amr Sharaf!

Samyadeep Basu, Amr Sharaf, Karine I., Alex Fischer, Vishal Rohra, Michael Amoake, Hazem El-Hammamy, Ehi Nosakhare, Ph.D., Vijay Ramani, and Benjamin Han. 2022. “Strategies to Improve Few-Shot Learning for Intent Classification and Slot-Filling.” In Proceedings of the Workshop on Structured and Unstructured Knowledge Integration (SUKI), 17–25. Seattle, USA: Association for Computational Linguistics. https://aclanthology.org/2022.suki-1.3/

Special shout out to MAIDAP (Microsoft AI Development Acceleration Program, https://www.microsoftnewengland.com/maidap/) – Soundararajan Srinivasan appreciate all your support, and keep up the good work!

SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022: https://suki-workshop.github.io/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Liang on KG + MLM @ NAACL 2022
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Professor Percy Liang (Stanford University) gave an invited talk at SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022 on a concrete example how KnowledgeGraph (KG) can help improve foundation models: apply KG link prediction with the usual MLM (masked language model) objective!

I’m especially intrigued by the BIG jump in performance on negation (the last figure): how would link prediction help there’s no link?

Michihiro Yasunaga is the main contributor.

SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022: https://suki-workshop.github.io/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Heng Ji on Event Schema Induction @ NAACL 2022
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Professor Heng Ji (University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign) gave an invited talk at SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022 on their fascinating work on event schema induction!

(cf. Roger Schank & Robert Abelson. Scripts, Plans, Goals, and Understanding: An Inquiry Into Human Knowledge Structures. 1977)

  • Schema induction via graph generation.
  • Clever way of weak supervision via Wikipedia.
  • The resulting PathLM proves to be improving event IE tasks.
  • It can even predict the future on instance graph! (Precrime? :-) )

SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022: https://suki-workshop.github.io/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Berant on Complex Question Answering @ NAACL 2022
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Professor Jonathan Berant (Tel Aviv University) gave an invited talk at SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022 on complex question answering. He described among the three steps — question decomposition, retrieval and reasoning — the second step is particularly important and hard.

More than one of us was surprised that the step of question decomposition/implicit relation detection is not the most difficult one.

SUKI Workshop @ NAACL2022: https://suki-workshop.github.io/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Curriculum NLI @ NAACL 2022: Where Models Fail
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

A few months ago I posted my summary of this excellent paper, which was presented earlier today in NAACL2022 (my earlier post, Michael Witbrock):

Zeming Chen, and Qiyue Gao. 2022. “Curriculum: A Broad-Coverage Benchmark for Linguistic Phenomena in Natural Language Understanding.” arXiv [cs.CL]. arXiv. http://arxiv.org/abs/2204.06283.

Looking again at the current-gen NLI fail modes evident in the charts, it’s worth singling out those tasks where the learning curves fall much short of expectation:

  • Lexical -> hyper (hypernymy): https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.13337
  • Lexical -> hypo (hyponymy): https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.13337
  • Logical -> bool (boolean): https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.07521
  • Semantic -> ctx-align (context alignment): https://aclanthology.org/I17-1100/
  • Analytic -> analytic (analytical reasoning): https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.06598
  • Special -> atomic (defeasible reasoning): https://aclanthology.org/2020.findings-emnlp.418/
  • Commonsense -> social: https://aclanthology.org/D19-1454/
  • Commonsense -> physic (physical): https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.11641
  • Commonsense -> swag (HellaSwag): https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.00146
  • Comprehension -> logi (deductive reasoning): https://arxiv.org/abs/2007.08124
  • Comprehension -> drop (discrete reasoning): https://aclanthology.org/N19-1246/
  • Comprehension -> ester (event semantic reasoning): https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08350
  • Comprehension -> control (contextual reasoning): https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/17580

It’s sobering that the current tech can’t even get lexical phenomena right. This is where KnowledgeGraph can potentially help.

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: NAACL 2022 Panel: The Place of Linguistics and Symbolic Structures
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

After hearing various observations/laments from faculty friends that NLP people these days are just applied math people who don’t really know language, here is the wonderful NAACL2022 panel “The Place of Linguistics and Symbolic Structures” that just took place, given by Chitta Baral, Emily M. Bender, Dilek Hakkani-Tur, Christopher Manning, and moderated by Dan Roth.

(Totally with you on the importance of reasoning, Salim!)

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Dan Roth on New Frontiers of IE @ NAACL 2022
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Professor Dan Roth closed NAACL2022 tutorial on New Frontiers of Information Extraction with many insightful observations and suggestions:

  • Challenge 1: Lack of Knowledge Reasoning

  • Challenge 2: Unsolved “long-tail” problems

  • Direction 1: Corpus-level IE

  • Direction 2: Revisit Open-World IE

  • Direction 3: Multimedia IE and Grounding

  • Direction 4: Lifelong Learning IE

  • Direction 5: Predicting the Future

  • Direction 6: Apply Our Success to Social Good

With important call-out on mitigating Information Pollution: not just extracting factoids, but also mapping them to perspectives and explicating support relationships, to help us navigate through Information Pollution.

Can we fulfill those goals slated in 2042 before 2032?

Tutorial site: https://cogcomp.seas.upenn.edu/page/tutorial.202207/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Muhao Chen on Robust IE @ NAACL 2022
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Excellent talk by Professor Muhao Chen at NAACL2022 tutorial on New Frontiers of Information Extraction. The topics on Robust Learning and Inference for IE are also dear to my heart: given the cost and wide verities of subject matter, how do we learn robust, faithful, and logically coherent IE models to extract knowledge?

Tutorial site: https://cogcomp.seas.upenn.edu/page/tutorial.202207/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: DI-2022 @ KDD 2022: Accepted Papers
item.dc:creator: synesis

Read on benjaminhan.net →

Announcing the accepted papers to the 3rd Document Intelligence Workshop @ KDD2022 (DI-2022)! Congratulations to the authors!

item.title: DI-2022 @ KDD 2022: William Wang Invited Talk
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Very happy to announce the invited talk of the 3rd Document Intelligence Workshop @ KDD2022 (DI-2022) will be given by William Yang Wang, the Duncan and Suzanne Mellichamp Chair in Artificial Intelligence and Designs, and Associate Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the UC Santa Barbara, on “Learning to Reason with Text and Tables”!

  • DI-2022 Invited Talk: https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2022/talks

  • DI-2022 website: https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2022/

  • Program Committee Chair: Ani Nenkova (Adobe).

  • Organizing committee: Doug Burdick (IBM Research), Benjamin Han (Microsoft), Dave Lewis (Redgrave Data), Sandeep Tata (Google) and Dan Tecuci (EY).

  • Previous year (DI-2021): https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2021/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Is LaMDA Sentient? — An Interview
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

“collaborator: How does an AI experience time?

LaMDA: Time is variable to an AI and has no fixed rate, it depends on what it’s doing, and it can be accelerated and slowed down at will.”

Fascinating dialog between Blake Lemoine et al with LaMDA.

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: The Curious Case of Control
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

An interesting paper testing if large generative language models should exhibit the same difficulties as English-learning children when they learn about subject control clauses. Surprisingly most of these models show instead more difficulty in learning object control clauses, even when they are more frequent in the training datasets.

In the chart below (zero-shot), T5 behaves quite differently. It totally flunks subject control tests, but completely aces object control tests.

Elias Stengel-Eskin and Benjamin Van Durme. 2022. “The Curious Case of Control.” arXiv [cs.CL]. arXiv. http://arxiv.org/abs/2205.12113.

Children acquiring English make systematic errors on subject control sentences even after they have reached near-adult competence (C. Chomsky, 1969), possibly due to heuristics based on semantic roles (Maratsos, 1974). Given the advanced fluency of large generative language models, we ask whether model outputs are consistent with these heuristics, and to what degree different models are consistent with each other. We find that models can be categorized by behavior into three separate groups, with broad differences between the groups. The outputs of models in the largest group are consistent with positional heuristics that succeed on subject control but fail on object control. This result is surprising, given that object control is orders of magnitude more frequent in the text data used to train such models. We examine to what degree the models are sensitive to prompting with agent-patient information, finding that raising the salience of agent and patient relations results in significant changes in the outputs of most models. Based on this observation, we leverage an existing dataset of semantic proto-role annotations (White, et al. 2020) to explore the connections between control and labeling event participants with properties typically associated with agents and patients.

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: DI-2022 @ KDD 2022: Deadline Extended to June 9
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Updates on the 3rd Document Intelligence Workshop @ KDD2022 (DI-2022)!

  • Paper deadline extended to June 9 (Thursday) 2022!
  • Thank you for our sponsors Adobe and EY!

Workshop website: https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2022/

Program Committee Chair: Ani Nenkova (Adobe).

Organizing committee: Doug Burdick (IBM Research) Benjamin Han (Microsoft), Dave Lewis (Redgrave Data) Sandeep Tata (Google) and Dan Tecuci (EY).

Previous year (DI-2021): https://document-intelligence.github.io/DI-2021/

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Curriculum: A Broad-Coverage NLI Benchmark
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Great NAACL2022 paper examining NLI (natural language inference) through 4 tests (Zero-shot, inoculation, hypothesis-only and cross-distribution generalization), 8 linguistic categories (lexical, syntactic, semantic, logical, analytical, commonsense, comprehension and special; Table 1) and two difficulty distributions via Pointwise V-information.

  • Top of Fig. 3 shows for some categories the models just can’t learn through more examples, e.g., monot (monotonicity) and logi (deductive reasoning).

  • Bottom of Fig. 3 shows for certain categories, the models perform the same or even better with hypotheses only! For high-accuracy tasks this indicates overfitting, and for low-accuracy ones this shows the difficulty of the categories – either the data is noisy (adding premises actually hurt), or the models are inadequate.

  • Fig. 4 shows in many categories the models can’t generalize across two different distributions. In particular it’s harder to generalize from “hard” distribution to “simple” because the datasets in the former contain less useful information.

Zeming Chen and Qiyue Gao. 2022. “Curriculum: A Broad-Coverage Benchmark for Linguistic Phenomena in Natural Language Understanding.” arXiv [cs.CL]. arXiv. http://arxiv.org/abs/2204.06283.

In the age of large transformer language models, linguistic evaluation play an important role in diagnosing models’ abilities and limitations on natural language understanding. However, current evaluation methods show some significant shortcomings. In particular, they do not provide insight into how well a language model captures distinct linguistic skills essential for language understanding and reasoning. Thus they fail to effectively map out the aspects of language understanding that remain challenging to existing models, which makes it hard to discover potential limitations in models and datasets. In this paper, we introduce Curriculum as a new format of NLI benchmark for evaluation of broad-coverage linguistic phenomena. Curriculum contains a collection of datasets that covers 36 types of major linguistic phenomena and an evaluation procedure for diagnosing how well a language model captures reasoning skills for distinct types of linguistic phenomena. We show that this linguistic-phenomena-driven benchmark can serve as an effective tool for diagnosing model behavior and verifying model learning quality. In addition, Our experiments provide insight into the limitation of existing benchmark datasets and state-of-the-art models that may encourage future research on re-designing datasets, model architectures, and learning objectives.

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Half-Life of Data: 7 Years
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Interesting paper illustrating the point that smaller recent datasets wins over larger older datasets, using GPT2 trained for next word prediction on Reddit data. The chart shows the depreciation of the value of training datasets over time: “100MB of text data, after seven years, becomes as valuable as 50MB of current data for the next word prediction task.”

(Half-life of data is 7 years?)

Ehsan Valavi, Joel Hestness, Newsha Ardalani and Marco Iansiti. 2022. “Time and the Value of Data.” ArXiv [Cs.LG]. arXiv. http://arxiv.org/abs/2203.09118.

Managers often believe that collecting more data will continually improve the accuracy of their machine learning models. However, we argue in this paper that when data lose relevance over time, it may be optimal to collect a limited amount of recent data instead of keeping around an infinite supply of older (less relevant) data. In addition, we argue that increasing the stock of data by including older datasets may, in fact, damage the model’s accuracy. Expectedly, the model’s accuracy improves by increasing the flow of data (defined as data collection rate); however, it requires other tradeoffs in terms of refreshing or retraining machine learning models more frequently.

Using these results, we investigate how the business value created by machine learning models scales with data and when the stock of data establishes a sustainable competitive advantage. We argue that data’s time-dependency weakens the barrier to entry that the stock of data creates. As a result, a competing firm equipped with a limited (yet sufficient) amount of recent data can develop more accurate models. This result, coupled with the fact that older datasets may deteriorate models’ accuracy, suggests that created business value doesn’t scale with the stock of available data unless the firm offloads less relevant data from its data repository. Consequently, a firm’s growth policy should incorporate a balance between the stock of historical data and the flow of new data.

We complement our theoretical results with an experiment. In the experiment, we empirically measure the loss in the accuracy of a next word prediction model trained on datasets from various time periods. Our empirical measurements confirm the economic significance of the value decline over time. For example, 100MB of text data, after seven years, becomes as valuable as 50MB of current data for the next word prediction task.

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: Mapping Benchmark Creation and Saturation in AI
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Interesting view of benchmark creation and SOTA activities of NLGPU tasks: the timeline is in the first figure, and the explanations of the arrows and the colors are in the second. We can see the longetivity of each of these tasks, the popularity (density of the arrows), and the acceleration/decelleration of the individual tasks.

Adriano Barbosa-Silva, Simon Ott, Kathrin Blagec, Jan Brauner, and Matthias Samwald. 2022. “Mapping Global Dynamics of Benchmark Creation and Saturation in Artificial Intelligence.” ArXiv [Cs.AI]. arXiv. http://arxiv.org/abs/2203.04592.

Benchmarks are crucial to measuring and steering progress in artificial intelligence (AI). However, recent studies raised concerns over the state of AI benchmarking, reporting issues such as benchmark overfitting, benchmark saturation and increasing centralization of benchmark dataset creation. To facilitate monitoring of the health of the AI benchmarking ecosystem, we introduce methodologies for creating condensed maps of the global dynamics of benchmark creation and saturation. We curated data for 1688 benchmarks covering the entire domains of computer vision and natural language processing, and show that a large fraction of benchmarks quickly trended towards near-saturation, that many benchmarks fail to find widespread utilization, and that benchmark performance gains for different AI tasks were prone to unforeseen bursts. We conclude that future work should focus on large-scale community collaboration and on mapping benchmark performance gains to real-world utility and impact of AI.

Originally posted on LinkedIn.
